## [00:00 - 03:01]

So the replenishment flow, which we'll cover now, is much like the customer winback flow that we just went over. But instead of the replenishment being for unengaged and lap subscribers like the customer winback was, replenishment flow is for more engaged and active subscribers. Essentially what the replenishment flow is, it's an email or a set of emails that remind people about products that they're either going to run out of or have already run out of. So for example, this is really great for consumable products, skincare, hair care, supplements. So say you purchased from a company some kind of supplement today. Typically if it's a 30 quantity supply, you should run out of about 30 days. So a company anywhere from about 25 to 30 days should be sending you an email saying, hey, here's a reminder of your product that's about to run out, please re-order and here's a discount to do that. The replenishment flow is so important because it helps drive repeat purchase. These emails are very timely and they're highly relevant. So again, if you've purchased a supplement from a company today and the supplement works, you're most likely going to want to re-order that. Not a whole lot of companies have subscriptions. So this makes shift for a subscription flow or instead of getting people to subscribe, you're just reminding them that they're about to run out and holding their hand to make sure that they order. The timing here is probably the most tricky and requires a lot of testing. But for example, say you're a coffee company and you supply 30 days amount of coffee for someone, if that person drinks one a day, on average, you should send that 30 days later. If someone drinks two a day, you should probably send it about two weeks later. So really depending on how your customers tend to use your product most frequently will depend on how and when you want to trigger these emails. And again, if you're not sure when to trigger these emails, it's probably better to create two or three emails in that flow and see how they perform. So for example, if you know someone's going to run out in 30 days, try sending some emails around day 21, so about three weeks after purchase, and see how people respond. Then send the second email after 30 days. So if you send it three weeks later, you'll send it one week after that. And then also try sending it after that 30 day window and see which one performs the best and how they perform. If you see a huge variance in performance, I would definitely recommend pausing or deleting the one that underperforms and keeping the other ones and continually test to find the optimal amount of time. One helpful tip that kind of sets up this replenishment flow and a lot of the other emails is telling people how frequently they should use your product and how they should use your product. So for example, we have clients that sell CBD oil and a lot of people are still unsure about how much to use and how to go about it. So by providing a recommended dosage, it gets people to get the maximum exposure and maximum benefit of the product. But it also allows you to sell and set the expectation of when you're going to send these replenishment emails. So for example, if you tell someone to take one drop of this CBD oil every single

day and they need to do it for 30 days, that makes it easy for you to send your replenishment email a couple days before that day 30. So I'm about to show you an example right now from Sephora. It's very simple about restocking your stash, letting them know that they're going to be running low on the product they ordered and showing the image of it with the button to restock now. And it does a couple other things. So at the very bottom, under the SPF, you'll see it shows five stars. So again, this reinforces that not only did you probably have a good experience with it, but so did others.
